evidence_quotes.quote: the server infrastructure was turned off by the developers.
I paid for a product that I still own a copy of, but it is unusable in any way as it was intended or advertised to be.
Blizzard's EULA explicitly states they can terminate the agreement (and thus your access) "at any time for any reason, or for no reason, with or without notice".

paragraphs.text: As a young adult, I would like to request and love to see the Digital Fairness Act being expanded to cover digital goods that were sold to consumers. In recent years, I experienced instances where I bought a digital copy of a game with required active online infrastructure provided by the Game developer or publisher company in order to work as advertised and as intended. After I paid the full price for a game, a period of time later, the server infrastructure was turned off by the developers. I paid for a product that I still own a copy of, but it is unusable in any way as it was intended or advertised to be. I am left without any support from the developer and publishers, as they decided to turn off the servers of login service for their game. Many game developers state openly that they can revoke your access to the game without giving any explanation or support after doing it even if you paid for their product. Blizzard's EULA explicitly states they can terminate the agreement (and thus your access) "at any time for any reason, or for no reason, with or without notice".
